text: The surgeon performs diagnostic laparoscopy, using laparoscopic instruments to reduce the omentum from the hernia sac. A monopolar current and blunt grasper are visible on the screen, aiding in the reduction process.

The surgeon uses a scale to measure the size of the hernia defect, preparing to place an appropriately sized mesh. A barb suture is visible as the mesh is positioned at the beginning of the procedure. The robotic system is then docked to commence dissection.

The surgeon uses a transabdominal retromuscular approach to divide the hernia sac, encountering thin and flimsy peritoneum. As the dissection progresses inferiorly, the peritoneum is either buttonholed or divided, leaving a hole where the sac was connected.

The surgical plane transitions into the space of Retzius in the midline and the space of Bogros laterally. The surgeon carefully dissects around the inferior epigastric vessels on the sides. As the dissection progresses lower, attention is given to avoiding the bladder and retro pubic veins.

The surgeon performs a gentle dissection in the correct plane, following loose areolar tissue to ensure smooth progress. Small vessels encountered are cauterized to achieve hemostasis and maintain a clear surgical field. A scale is used to measure the defect, which is approximately four by five centimeters. A tailored mesh, measuring thirteen by fifteen centimeters, is placed to provide adequate overlap and occupy the space effectively.

The surgeon places the mesh in the proposed location after completing approximately 80-90% of the dissection during robotic surgery. Additional work is performed in specific areas to create a precise space, ensuring the mesh remains stable post-surgery.

The surgeon creates a well-defined space for the mesh placement, ensuring it sits without creases. Hemostasis is achieved in the surgical field, and the surgeon notes that the plane on the right side could have been improved, as it was positioned higher than intended.

The surgeon navigates the surgical field using robotic instruments, re-entering the correct anatomical plane while preserving the inferior epigastric vessels. Small blood vessels are divided, and a lump of preperitoneal fat is encountered, herniating through a defect likely related to a previous cesarean section.

The surgeon transitions from the space of Phrygius to the space of Bogros, carefully creating an adequate but not overly large dissection area. Once satisfied with the hemostasis and the positioning of the mesh, the surgeon prepares to close the hernia defect.

The surgeon uses a barbed suture to secure the tissues, ensuring they do not slip back. The surgeon refines the area where the mesh will rest, creating a smooth plane in the retropubic area to prevent the mesh from tenting or folding. The suturing is performed using a backhand technique.

The surgeon closes the hernia defect with a strong, snug suture, ensuring a non-strangulating closure. The absorbable suture is used to prevent the mesh from protruding into the hernia, allowing time for scar tissue to form and reinforce the repair. The closure is completed, and the surgical field is prepared for mesh placement.

The surgeon places orientation stitches using a 3-0 Vicryl suture to mark the midline for mesh placement. The mesh is aligned with the body's midline and fixed behind the pubic arch, ensuring symmetrical placement.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection in an area with minimal vascular and nerve presence, ensuring safe fixation of the mesh. A stitch is placed to secure the mesh, preventing movement, before proceeding with the closure of the PRS Peritoneum Complex.

The surgeon uses a III-O Stratafix barbed suture to close the defect, taking two to three consecutive bites before pulling to achieve optimal alignment. The closure crosses the midline and continues to the opposite side, addressing small buttonholes and the herniated defect before completing the peritoneal closure.

The surgeon addresses small defects in the surgical area, opting to close the main incision first before attending to defects larger than five to eight millimeters. Using figure-of-eight or simple sutures, the surgeon ensures that no bowel or omentum can enter these defects, preventing post-operative complications.
